models are not good at capturing long-distance context dependencies within RS images, negatively impacting their performance.
With the appearance of the visual transformer, the above problems have been mitigated. However, the high time costs of the
transformer-based models limit their applicability. In addition,
previous CD networks (whether CNN-based or transform-based)
do not pay attention to the edges of changed areas, reducing the
quality of change maps. To overcome the shortcomings discussed
above, we propose a new CD method named edge-assisted
adaptive transformer detector (EATDer). EATDer consists of a
Siamese encoder and an edge-aware decoder. Each branch in the
Siamese encoder encloses three self-adaption vision transformer
(SAVT) blocks, which aim to capture the local and global
information within RS images. Also, two branches are connected
by full-range fusion modules (FRFMs), which focus on ...

I. I NTRODUCTION
HANKS to the development of sensor technologies,
a large number of remote sensing (RS) images can be
collected every day. These Earth observation data provide rich
land-cover information for researchers. As a basic and vibrant
interpretation technique, RS change detection (RSCD) plays a
vital role in the RS community. It aims to find the changed

disaster monitoring [5], and scene understanding [6], [7],
[8], [9].
In the past, numerous methods have been developed for
RSCD with the help of traditional machine learning [10],
[11]. Those methods mainly rely on algebra and transformation

The goals of FRFMs are to mine the invisible temporal
knowledge among different RS images and to emphasize the
changed/unchanged areas. The decoder contains a restoring
block and a refining block. Their targets are to integrate
and enhance the multiscale features obtained from encoders
and produce the change maps. Meanwhile, an auxiliary edge
detection task is added to ensure the decoder is aware of the
edges of changed areas so that the change maps’ quality can

RSCD. Chen et al. [42] proposed a bi-temporal image transformer (BIT) to handle the RSCD problem by exploring
the spatial–temporal contexts. BIT first maps RS images
into high-level feature maps using two conventional CNNs.
Then, it adopts a transformer encoder to model the contextual
relations in both spatial and temporal domains. Finally, BIT
leverages two transformer encoders to produce the refined
feature maps, which can be used to decide change maps.

B. Transformer-Based CD Models
Transformer, proposed in natural language processing [22],
has become popular in the RS community due to its intrinsic
capacity in long-range relations exploration [39], [40], [41].
For RSCD, many transformer-based methods have been proposed, and we broadly divide them into two classes.
The methods within the first-class focus on taking advantage of the original transformer to design new models for

each of them involves three SAVT blocks to learn valuable
information from bi-temporal RS images with a tolerable computational cost. In addition, two networks are connected by our
FRFM. In this way, not only can the profitable information
corresponding to bi-temporal RS images be transferred, but

Introduction
We select three CD datasets to evaluate the proposed EATDer, including the LEVIR building CD dataset
(LEVIR-CD) [58], the CD dataset (CDD) proposed in [59],
and the Wuhan University CD dataset (WHU) [60].
LEVIR-CD contains 637 Google Earth image patch pairs
with a size of 1024 × 1024 pixels. The pixel resolution of
those images is 0.5 m. LEVIR-CD mainly focuses on the
changes in various buildings, such as villa residences and tall
apartments. The original dataset is divided into 445 training
pairs, 128 testing pairs, and 64 validation pairs. However,
considering the limited computational capacity, we crop the

The experiments are conducted using the Pytorch platform
on a workstation with two GeForce RTX 3090 GPUs which
have 24G Memory and 35.6 Tera floating point operations. Our
EATDer is initialized with Xavier [61] to avoid the gradient
vanishing and gradient explosion. The AdamW algorithm is
adopted to optimize the parameters of our model. The l...

Introduction
We select three CD datasets to evaluate the proposed EATDer, including the LEVIR building CD dataset
(LEVIR-CD) [58], the CD dataset (CDD) proposed in [59],
and the Wuhan University CD dataset (WHU) [60].
LEVIR-CD contains 637 Google Earth image patch pairs
with a size of 1024 × 1024 pixels. The pixel resolution of
those images is 0.5 m. LEVIR-CD mainly focuses on the
changes in various buildings, such as villa residences and tall
apartments. The original dataset is divided into 445...

B. Experimental Settings
The experiments are conducted using the Pytorch platform
on a workstation with two GeForce RTX 3090 GPUs which
have 24G Memory and 35.6 Tera floating point operations. Our
EATDer is initialized with Xavier [61] to avoid the gradient
vanishing and gradient explosion. The AdamW algorithm is
adopted to optimize the parameters of our model. The learning
rate, training epochs, and batch size are set to 0.001, 200,
and 22. Note that we use the dilatant/original edge detection
...

As discussed in Section III-B1, SAVT is similar to PVT
V2 [53], which focuses on reducing the computational costs
of the transformer by decreasing the feature maps’ spatial
sizes. However, there are two different points between SAVT
and them. First, PVT V2 reduces the size of K and V to
a fixed scale. Compared to it, the reduced size of K and V
is adaptively decided by SAVT. The benefit of doing this is
that it reduces the complexity of parameter tuning based on
the size of input data and the re...

we can find that EATDer outperforms EATDer-PVTv2 in
all cases. This indicates that compared to a fixed dimension
reduction mode, the adaptive dimension reduction strategy
in SAVT can better adapt to the RSCD task. Admittedly,
the reduced dimension (a free setting) of PVT V2 may be
improper for our tasks, and we can find an empirically optimal
dimension by careful tuning. However, the adaptive dimension
reduction scheme makes SAVT more extensive compared with
complicated tuning processes.
In the ...

edge-ware decoder consists of a restoring block and a refining
block, which focus on fusing the multiscale features and
enhancing their discrimination. Taking the edges of changed
areas into account, both change and edge detection results
are output by the decoder. Along with the joint loss function,
high-quality change maps can be produced. The encouraging
results counted on three widespread datasets confirm that the
proposed EATDer is applicable to RSCD.

paragraphs.text:
Abstract— Cross-modal remote-sensing image–text retrieval
(CMRSITR) is a challenging topic in the remote-sensing (RS)
community. It has gained growing attention because it can be
flexibly used in many practical applications. In the current
deep era, with the help of deep convolutional neural networks
(DCNNs), many successful CMRSITR

methods.content_preview:
methods have been proposed. Most of them first learn valuable features from RS
images and texts, respectively. Then, the obtained visual and
textual features are mapped into a common space for the
final retrieval. The above operations are feasible; however, two
difficulties are still to be solved. One is that the semantics
within the visual and textual features are misaligned due to the
independent learning manner. The other one is that the deep
links between RS images and texts cannot be fully explored
by simple common space mapping. To overcome the above
challenges, we propose a new model named interacting-enhancing
feature transformer (IEFT) for CMRSITR, which regards the RS
images and texts as a whole. First, a simple feature embedding
module (FEM) is developed to map images and texts into
the visual and textual feature spaces. Second, an information
interacting-enhancing module (IIEM) is designed to simultaneously model the inner relationships between RS images and texts
and enhan...

II. R ELATED W ORK
A. Cross-Modal Image–Text Retrieval
Cross-modal image–text retrieval refers to finding semantically similar data between images and texts. According to
the type of images, we review some published works from
the following two aspects, including cross-modal natural scene
image-text retrieval (CMNSITR) and CMRSITR.
1) Cross-Modal Natural Scene Image–Text Retrieval:
CMNSITR is always a hot research topic in image processing,
which aims to find semantically similar data across images and
texts [31], [32]. Especially in the current deep era, the rapid
development of CMNSITR is obvious to all [33]. As early as
2018, You et al. [34] developed an end-to-end semantic embedding model for CMNSITR. This method leverages DCNNs to
learn hierarchical features from both images and texts first.
Then, the local information (within the middle-level features)
and the semantic messages (within the high-level features)

To train our IEFT using the obtained matching scores,
we first construct positive and negative samples for each RS
image. In particular, we treat an RS image and its paired texts
as positive samples. At the same time, the RS image and
λ random texts are regarded as negative samples. Then, the
cross-entropy loss function is selected to minimize the gaps
between Si and the relationship label m, that is,
Lmatching = CrossEntropy(Si , m).

In the testing stage, we first count the matching scores
between the query (an RS image or a text) and all candidates
(texts or RS images in the archive). Then, the retrieval list can
be determined in descending order of scores.
IV. E XPERIMENTS AND D ISCUSSION
A. Testing Datasets

introduction.content_preview:
Introduction
Four popular RS image datasets are employed to evaluate
our IEFT, including the RSICD [60], RSITMD [16], UCMCaptions [61], and Sydney-Captions [61] datasets.
The RSICD dataset is a large-scale RS image caption
dataset, containing 10 921 RS images with varied pixel resolutions and fixed sizes (224 × 224). These RS images are
divided into 30 categories, and the number of RS images
per class varies from 240 to 1031. Also, each RS image

has five description sentences in RSICD. RSITMD is a
derivate of RSICD, with 4743 256 × 256 RS images. Parts
are selected from the RSICD dataset, and Google Earth
collects others. There are 32 classes in this dataset, and the
numbers of RS images within each class are imbalanced.
Like RSICD, each RS image in RSITMD has five textual
captions. The UCM-Captions dataset was constructed based
on the UC Merced Land Use dataset [62]. UCM-Captions
contains 2100 RS images and are divided into 21 semantic
classes equally. Their pixel resolutions and siz...

D. Ablation Study
As exhibited in Fig. 1, our IEFT involves an FEM, an IIEM,
and an RM. Among the three modules, IIEM is the most
complex one. It consists of three FIE blocks, each of which
mainly contains an IMRI subblock and a VFE subblock. The
IMRI subblock aims at modeling the relationships between
RS images and texts by SAM. The VFE subblock focuses
on enhancing the RS images’ representations by exploring the
channelwise and localwise knowledge hidden in images, which
is achieved by the CWF...

on the basis of Net-3. Actually, Net-4 is our IEFT. The

Abstract—Remote sensing visual grounding (RSVG) is a lively

results.content_preview:
performance of transformers in cross-modal interaction and alignment,
they have dominated the RSVG community, and numerous
transformer-based methods have been developed. Although they
have achieved success in various applications, their performance
in visual and textual feature alignment still does not reach the
level we expected. In addition, these methods place relatively
limited emphasis on bounding box coordinate generation, where
directly predicting coordinates through a decoder and grounding
head can result in suboptimal localization accuracy. To overcome
the above limitations, this article presents a new RSVG method,
named language-guided progressive query refinement with diffusion model for visual grounding (LPQR-DVG). It contains a
multiscale text-guided feature learning module (MTFLM) and an
iterative query localization module (IQLM). MTFLM integrates
text guidance into all stages of the visual feature extraction
process through cross-attention layers, effectively incorporati...

Index Terms—Diffusion model, language guidance, progressive
reasoning, remote sensing (RS), visual grounding.

I. I NTRODUCTION
EMOTE sensing visual grounding (RSVG) is a lively
research topic in the RS community. It focuses on
localizing the referred objects within RS images according
to the textual descriptions [1], [2], which can be viewed as
an extension of RS object detection [3]. Recently, RSVG
has received growing attention owing to the following reasons. On the one hand, understanding RS images at the
scene level becomes increasingly important as the resolution
increases [4], [5], [6]. Detecting ...

objects in RS images often lack clear boundaries and exhibit
intricate spatial relationships. Also, these targets are diverse in
scale, ranging from small isolated objects (e.g., “tiny vehicle”)
to large complex areas (e.g., “expressway service area”). Our
LPQR-DVG is developed to overcome these challenges. In
detail, we introduce a progressive diffusion-denoising process
withi...

Fig. 1. Pipeline of LPQR-DVG. It consists of an MTFLM and an IQLM.
MTFLM aims to extract and integrate visual and text features effectively.
IQLM pays attention to deeply interacting with two types of features and
locating the targets precisely. In addition, a progressive diffusion learning
strategy is introduced to train LPQR-DVG for accurate visual grounding.

Fig. 2. Illustration of MTFLM. It consists of a text-guided visual feature
extractor and a textual feature extractor. The textual feature extractor is
responsible for extracting text features and projecting them to multiple
scales to match the multiscale visual features produced by the text-guided
visual feature extractor, as well as the final visual output. The text-guided
visual feature extractor leverages textual guidance to extract text-aware visual
features and employs a transformer encoder...

IV. E XPERIMENTS
A. Datasets Description
To testify to the effectiveness and robustness of our method,
we select two popular datasets, including DIOR-RSVG [2] and
OPT-RSVG [1]. The DIOR-RSVG dataset was constructed
by researchers from Northwestern Polytechnical University
in 2023. It is an extension of the DIOR dataset [53].
DIOR-RSVG contains 17 402 high-resolution RS scenes....

B. Experimental Settings
All experiments are conducted on a workstation equipped
with a GeForce RTX 3090 with 24 GB of memory. The
parameters of the ResNet backbone, transformer encoder, and
textual backbone in MTFLM are initialized using the pretrained weights in DETR [38] and BERT [45]. Other parts
of our LPQR-DVG are initialized randomly. The channel
multipliers in the RSVG-oriented reversed U-Net are used
to scale the number of channels at each layer. Specifically,
we initialize the model wi...

D. Ablation Studies
As introduced in Section III, the main parts of our
LPQR-DVG are MTFLM, IQLM, and the diffusion training
algorithm. Meanwhile, the key components in MTFLM and
IQLM are the multiscale text guidance and the PQR unit.
To investigate their contributions to LPQR-DVG, this section
outlines the design of the ablation experiments.
First, we fix the diffusion training algorithm and examine
the importance of multiscale text guidance and the PQR unit.
To this end, the multiscale text guidance would be removed
from LPQR-DVG, and the PQR unit would also be replaced
with a standard cross-attention transformer unit. Note that
only one of the two steps above is executed at a time. The
results of different LPQR-DVGs counted on the two datasets
are summarized in Table III. For clarity, we report their
Pr @0.5 and mIoU values exclusively. When the multiscale
text guidance and PQR unit are ablated (see the first row),
LPQR-DVG’s pe...

E. Further Analysis of MTFLM
Based on the ablation study results (see Section IV-D),
the usefulness of multiscale text guidance in MTFLM is

This article proposes a new model for RSVG tasks named
LPQR-DVG. It primarily consists of an MTFLM module
and an IQLM module. MTFLM is designed to continuously
guide visual features with textual information throughout the
feature extraction process, ensuring more robust and contextually aligned interactions between visual and text features.
IQLM is developed to enable sufficient interaction between
queries, visual, and text features during the training process in the RSVG-oriented reversed U-Net...

enhance interaction with textual features. Second, compared
with existing methods, LPQR-DVG still shows limitations in
terms of model size and inference speed. Therefore, further
improving the model’s performance while reducing its size
and enhancing inference speed will be the focus of our future
research.

results.content_preview:
performance cannot reach what we expected due to inadequate
cross-modal feature learning and simple feature fusion. This
article proposes a multiple information collaborative fusion
network (MICF-Net) to overcome those limitations, which aims
to leverage the essentially consistent spatial relationships and
high-level semantic information in multimodal data to guide
the extraction of multimodal fusion features. Specifically, MICFNet first uses a simple two-branch convolutional neural network
(CNN) for preliminary feature extraction. Then, a dual-branch
cross-modal attention fusion transformer (CMAFT) is developed
to mine global contextual content. By fusing the attention maps
of two modalities and limiting their similarity, CMAFT can
retain modality-specific information while achieving information
interaction based on spatial relationships. Next, an adaptive
mask modulation (AMM) module is designed to dynamically

effectiveness of ...

light detection and ranging (LiDAR), multimodal remote
sensing (RS).

I. I NTRODUCTION
N RECENT years, remote sensing (RS) technology has
been developed rapidly, and RS imagery plays an irreplaceable role in various applications, such as disaster detection,
agricultural management, and urban development planning [1],
[2], [3]. With the emergence of various RS data, multimodal
learning has gradually attracted researchers’ attention. The
land covers within RS images can be fully captured by
integrating information from multiple perspectives. However,
massive data fr...

methods.content_preview:
model’s learning burden, as they contain complementary clues
and redundant and interfering messages. In addition, due to the
diverse imaging conditions, the differences within various RS
data are significant, quickly causing an imbalance problem in
model learning. Therefore, preserving valuable information,
eliminating unnecessary knowledge, and considering the
specific characteristics of different RS data are crucial to RS
multimodal learning [4], [5].
Hyperspectral images (HSIs), as an important product of
RS data, have always garnered increasing attention. Due to
hundreds of continuous spectral bands, HSIs can capture
subtle differences among targets to accurately identify ground
objects [6]. However, it encounters challenges in expressing
differences between objects of the same material and is
susceptible to external factors, such as cloud cover [7].
To overcome these limitations, many other kinds of RS
data have been introduced to the HSI community [8],
including multispectral ima...

technologies incorporated in them can be broadly categorized
into feature fusion and information interaction [18]. For
feature-fusion-based technologies, they can be further divided

previous work, some
challenges still deserve further research. First, for HSI
and LiDAR joint classification tasks, information interaction
during the feature extraction process is crucial to enhance
the representation ability of features [27], [28]. However,
existing information interaction methods often deal with
features directly, which may weaken the specific information
of each modality. Second, the feature learning rates for
different modalities are always imbalanced due to the data
differences. This leads to insufficient feature extraction in
weaker modalities, which harms the representation ability of
final features [29], [30]. Last but not least, simple feature
fusion strategies struggle to fully exploit the correlation and
complementary information of heterogeneous features from
different modalities, limiting the model’s performance [31],
[32], [33].

Abstract— As a content management technique, remote sensing
(RS) scene classification (RSSC) always attracts researchers’
attention. In the past decades, many successful

methods.content_preview:
methods have
been proposed. Nevertheless, their prerequisite is that there are
large labeled datasets, which is a strict demand in practice.
To resolve this contradiction, developing RSSC models with the
help of few-shot learning (FSL) has become popular. Due to
lacking prior knowledge, most of the existing few-shot RSSC
models pay attention to the learning algorithm. However, they do
not attach importance to the complex contents within RS scenes
and the intricate interclass/intraclass relations between RS scenes.
This would influence their

performance negatively. In this article,
we propose a new few-shot RSSC model named multipretext-task
prototypes guided dynamic contrastive learning network (MPCLNet). MPCL-Net consists of a multipretext tasks generation
submodule, a deep feature learning submodule, and a joint
optimization submodule. First, two RS-oriented pretext tasks are
constructed under the self-supervised learning (SSL) framework
in the multipretext tasks generation submodule, which aims to
explore multiscale and rotation-invariant information from RS
scenes. Second, a simple convolutional neural network (CNN) is
developed in the deep feature learning submodule to transform
the RS scenes into visual features. Third, three loss functions are
formulated and integrated into the joint optimization submodule.
Their goals are to fully capture the diverse land covers within
RS scenes and compact/separate the intraclass/interclass samples
with limited supervision. Finally, our MPCL-Net can be trained
in a meta way. The ...

remote sensing scene classification (RSSC), multipretext tasks
self-supervised learning (SSL).

EMOTE sensing (RS) scene classification (RSSC) is an
important research topic in the RS community, which
aims to assign semantic labels to RS scenes according to
their contents [1], [2]. As a fundamental technique, RSSC
is widely used in various RS applications, such as change
detection [3], [4], contents mining [5], [6], geospatial object
detection [7], [8], and so on. With the rapid development
of deep learning, many RSSC methods have been proposed
based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs)...

II. R ELATED W ORK
A. Deep Remote Sensing Scene Classification Model
With the help of CNNs, a large number of RSSC models
have been proposed in recent years. This section divides them
into two groups for a short review. In the first group, the
methods focus on improving the classification performance by
reducing the influence of intraclass variance and interclass similarity in RS scenes. For example, Cheng et al. [27] proposed
a discriminative CNN (D-CNN) to complete the RSSC tasks.
Apart from normal cross-entropy loss, the metric-learning loss
is developed in D-CNN to ensure the samples from the same
(different) classes can be close to (far away from) each other.
The positive results illustrate the superiorities of D-CNN. Similarly, another metric-learning-based deep model was proposed
in [28]. Based on the Siamese network, Chopra et al. [29]
design a metric learning regularization layer to enlarge or
decrease the distances between RS scenes fr...

Following the most commonly used meta-learning protocol,
we perform five-way one-shot and five-way five-shot classification. We implement our model in the PyTorch platform4 and
run our experiment on two Amax GeForce RTX 3090 GPUs.

model. The initial learning rate is set as 1 × 10−4 and the

introduction of SSL and FSL ideas, MPCL-Net can be trained in a
meta way. On the other hand, since involving the multiview
scheme and the contrastive learning theory, MPCL-Net can
not only cope with the interclass/intraclass issue but also
distinguish the unseen classes. The encouraging experimental
results on three public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness
and generalization for few-shot RSSC tasks.

paragraphs.text:
Abstract— Remote sensing (RS) scene classification (RSSC) is
a prominent research topic in the RS community. Multilevel
feature fusion is an important way of addressing RSSC, and
many

methods.content_preview:
methods have been proposed in recent years. Although they
succeed, current methods can still be improved, particularly in
distinguishing the contributions of different multilevel features
and fully and effectively fusing them. To address the above
issues and fully exploit the potential of multilevel features for
RSSC tasks, we propose a new model named multiscale sparse
cross-attention network (MSCN). It not only focuses on the effectiveness of feature learning but also emphasizes the rationality of
feature fusion. In detail, MSCN first extracts multilevel features
using a pretrained ResNet50. Also, these features are divided
into high- and low-level features according to the clues they are
involved. Then, a multiscale sparse cross-attention (MSC) module
is developed to cross-fuse the high-level feature with various
low-level features, thereby effectively mining helpful information
from multilevel features. In the fusion process, MSC not only
explores the multiscale messages in RS scen...

to assigning the specific semantics to RS images according to their scene-level contents. It is an important research
topic in the RS image processing community, and it plays
a crucial role in various applications, including land use
investigation, environmental protection, and so on [1], [2], [3].

discussion above, a useful RSSC model should be
capable of capturing multiscale messages from the RS image,
effectively integrating them, and suppressing the influence of
“irrelevant” information. These are precisely the key issues
that the proposed MSCN aims to address.
B. Multilevel Feature Fusion for RSSC
Multilevel feature fusion is a helpful technique for RSSC,
in which the features from different scales are integrated
subtly to describe the complex contents hidden in RS images.
With the assistance of these intact representations, good RSSC
results can be achieved. In this section, we divide the popular
methods into two groups for review, i.e., feature-combinationand CAM-based models.
Feature-combination-based fusion methods combine different features according to some specific rules, such as
concatenation, addition, and multiplication. For example,
Bi et al. [19] proposed a multiple instance densely connected


convolution network (MIDC-Net) for RSSC. MIDC-Net first
develops a de...

introduction.content_preview:
Introduction
This article selects three public datasets to study the usefulness of MSCN.
1) UCM Land Use Dataset: The UC Merced (UCM)
dataset [18] was constructed and released by the UC Merced
computer vision laboratory. It encompasses 21 categories,
such as “agricultural,” “dense residential,” and “storage tanks.”
There are 100 RS scenes in each category. Their spatial sizes
and pixel resolutions are 256 × 256 and 1 foot. Examples of
the UCM dataset are depicted in Fig. 7. To comprehensively
evaluate our model’s performance, we randomly select 50%
and 80% RS scenes from UCM to establish the training sets,
and the remaining RS scenes are regarded as the testing sets.
2) Aerial Image Dataset: The Aerial Image Dataset (AID)
[57] was constructed jointly by Wuhan University and
Huazhong University of Science and Technology in 2017.
It comprises 30 classes, each of which contains approximately
220 to 420 RS scenes, resulting in a total of 10 000 scenes.
Their spatial sizes are 600 × 600, an...

B. Experimental Settings
All experiments are conducted on a workstation equipped
with a GeForce RTX 3090 GPU, using the PyTorch platform
with a version of 1.13.1. ResNet50 of MSCN is initialized
using the pretrained parameters from ImageNet [58], and the
other parts are initialized randomly. We select AdamW [59]
as the optimizer to train the proposed MSCN. The epochs
and batch size are set to be 200 and 128, respectively. The
first five epochs are warm-up processes, and their learning
rate equal...

C. Parameters Analysis
Several free parameters in our MSCN should be set in
advance, they are the pooling window sizes in MSC, the
threshold k1 and k2 in the TopK operation, and the group
number g in GCE. In this section, we aim to empirically find
their optical values for the three datasets.
1) Analysis of Pooling Window Sizes in MSC: As mentioned in Section III-B, MSC applies three average pooling
operations with different window sizes to the input data to
explore the multiscale clues. To find...

we set the ranges of k1 and k2 as [(1/2), (1/4), (1/8), (1/16)]
and [(1/3), (1/6), (1/9)]. The classification results of different
MSCNs with various k1 and k2 across the three datasets are
shown in Fig. 11.
We can observe that classification accuracies decrease for
the UCM and AID datasets as the value of k1 decreases. When
k2 is greater than (1/6), the experimental results increase as the
value of k2 decreases. Conversely, when the value of k2 continuously decreases, the accuracies would decli...

E. Ablation Study
MFCM and GCE are two key parts of the proposed
MSCN. MFCM is mainly composed of three MSCs, which
are designed to extract valuable clues from multilevel features.
GCE is an enhancer that effectively highlights key information
within the classification feature. In this section, we plan
to study their contributions. To this end, we construct four
networks and analyze their performance on the three datasets.
These four networks are as follows:
1) Net-0: MFEM + CM;
2) Net-1: MFEM +...

that these two modules can provide complementary clues for
the final classification.
Additionally, to further demonstrate the effectiveness of our
model, we designed the following ablation experiments, they
are the following:
1) Net-4: MFEM + MFCM(CAM) + GCE + CM;
2) Net-5: MFEM + MFCM(only k1 in MSC) + GCE +
CM;
3) Net-6: MFEM + MFCM(only k2 in MSC) + GCE +
CM;
4) Net-7: MFEM + MFCM + CBAM + CM.

evaluate the performance of MSC compared to CAM....

abstract.content_preview:
Abstract: Image-based geo-localization focuses on predicting the geographic information of query
images by matching them with annotated images in a database. To facilitate relevant studies, researchers collect numerous images to build the datasets, which explore many challenges faced in
real-world geo-localization applications, significantly improving their practicability. However, a
crucial challenge that often arises is overlooked, named the cross-time challenge in this paper, i.e.,
if query and database images are taken from the same landmark but at different time periods, the
significant difference in their image content caused by the time gap will notably increase the difficulty
of image matching, consequently reducing geo-localization accuracy. The cross-time challenge has a
greater negative influence on non-real-time geo-localization applications, particularly those involving
a long time span between query and database images, such as satellite-view geo-localization. Furthermore...

introduction.content_preview:
Introduction

Revised: 18 August 2023

Image-based geo-localization is an important topic in the field of remote sensing
(RS) image processing, which focuses on using image matching technology to predict the
landmark information of a given query image. In recent years, it has been applied in various
real-world fields, such as agriculture [1], aircraft navigation [2], event detection [3], drone
delivery [4], and so on. Generally, image-based geo-localization is regarded as a subtask of
image retrieval [5–7], where similar images are retrieved from a database annotated with
landmark information (e.g., name or coordinates), providing the corresponding information
for the query image [8–14]. Therefore, the difficulty lies in the significant difference between
query images and the annotated images within a database, caused by various perspectives,
objects, environments, etc. Global positioning system (GPS), as an alternative localization
technology, also finds extensive applications across ...

Furthermore, since GPS-based localization technologies require an active online connection,
they cannot work in offline scenarios without access to real-time GPS data. Therefore, the
study of image-based geo-localization tasks is crucial in addressing these limitations and
advancing the related fields.

also provides the latitude and longitude coordinates necessary for conducting accurate
geo-localization studies. More details will be introduced in Section 3.
In summary, the four main contributions of this paper are as follows:
1.

We propose a MTGL40-5 dataset for conducting a satellite-view geo-localization task,
which consists of query images and a database featuring RS images acquired from
satellites.
MTGL40-5 not only explores the cross-time challenge by providing images from
40 geographic locations spanning five different years, but also provides precise latitude
and longitude coordinates for studying high-accuracy geo-localization tasks.

Related Work
In this section, we will provide a brief review about existing geo-localization studies
focusing on two aspects: datasets and methods.
2.1. Existing Datasets for Geo-Localization
At first, ground-view-based studies gained popularity, resulting in the creation of
numerous datasets that included both query images and a corresponding database. For
example, Jan et al. [25] presented a dataset consisting of 17K images from Google Street
View, capturing the central area of Paris, to facilitate landmark location recognition. In
general, the larger the dataset volume, the higher the geo-localization performance. Therefore, a larger dataset [21] was introduced, which comprises 1.7M images annotated with
landmark names, thereby providing a robust benchmark for geo-localization. In addition
to the dataset’s volume, diversity also plays a crucial role in ensuring its robustness. In
the literature [35], a dataset was proposed that specifically aims to enhance diversity by
expanding the...

2.1. Existing Datasets for Geo-Localization
At first, ground-view-based studies gained popularity, resulting in the creation of
numerous datasets that included both query images and a corresponding database. For
example, Jan et al. [25] presented a dataset consisting of 17K images from Google Street
View, capturing the central area of Paris, to facil...

consequently reducing the flexibility of adjusting the camera angle. Therefore, in the Earth
Observation Center (EOC) dataset [38], multi-angle UAV images were collected from a low
altitude of 340 m above sea level, which includes not only the typical top-down angles
but also various side angles of buildings. To further enhance angle diversity, Zheng et al.
presented an extensive collection of UAV images known as the University-1652 dataset [19],
which encompasses 1652 buildings across 72 univer...

challenges posed by view diversity. Additionally, as a frequently used technology for
improving feature discrimination, the attention mechanism is often employed to selectively extract crucial features from images [4,51], thereby enhancing the performance of
cross-view geo-localization models.
3. MTGL40-5 Dataset
3.1. Dataset Description
Based on Google Earth (Google Inc., Mountain View, CA, USA), we create a comprehensive dataset for studying the cross-time satellite-view geo-localization, wher...

Collecting large-scale images: Large-scale images are collected by first searching
Wikipedia for numerous ports and airports located in different countries, and their
corresponding latitude and longitude coordinates are recorded. Secondly, the historical image data of the selected locations on Google Maps are reviewed to ensure that
images spanning at least five years can be obtained. Thirdly, 40 target locations are
selected which satisfy this criterion. Finally, based on Google Maps, the image...

We randomly select a location as the sample and show its five images captured from
different years in Figure 1, and display some split patch images in Figure 2.
To further show the characteristics of our dataset, we compare it with the existing six
datasets University-1652 [19], CVUSA [30], CVACT [29], Lin et al. [14], Tian et al. [9], and
Vo et al. [39] from seven aspects: “training set size”, “target”, “multi-temporal”, “largescale”, “GPS coordinates”, “multi-angle”, and “evaluation” in Table ...

Figure 1. Satellite-view RS image display of airports, where original images and key images are
shown in the first and second rows, respectively. The original images were collected by Google Maps,
and are available from five distinct years. These images contain the complete content of the location,
with an image size of 18,735 × 16,523. The key images contain the key region of the original images,
with an image size of 9500 × 7500. The GPS coordinates of the center point of the original images a...

Figure 2. Split patch image display. Each group consists of five RS image patches, displaying a small
area’s images from five different years. We can find that the position of the aircraft in the patch, the
color features of the landform, and the position of the ships in the same port also change with time,
which means that in a multi-temporal dataset, capturing the features of different phases of the same
location has a significant impact on the geographic localization research.

Multi-temporal: MTGL40-5 is a dataset consisting of multiple geographic locations,
where each location includes images captured in different years, enabling the observation of dynamic changes in various landmarks over time. When training models use
this dataset, many image pairs taken from the same landmark but in different years
allow models to learn about the time-invariant features.
Large-scale: MTGL40-5 comprises RS images acquired at a spatial resolution of 0.5 m,
with each original image c...

Accurate labels: MTGL40-5 records the latitude and longitude coordinates for the four
vertices and center point of each RS image in the database, which is important for
performing accurate geo-localization.

Finally, an important usage notice of MTGL40-5 needs to be explained: training the
model with cross-time data (the query and database images are from different years) rather
than same-time data. To train a model for satellite-view geo-localization, it is important
to ensure that the feature distance between the query and database images from the same
landmark is reduced, but not to the extent of reaching zero (i.e., overfitting). However, when
the query and database images come from the same tim...

(3) Since the database images are extracted from the original image, each database
image can be assigned an index corresponding to its original image. Therefore, when we
obtain image pairs of query and database images, N query images extracted from a key
image will correspond to N original image indexes. Finally, the key image can be assigned
an original image index through a voting mechanism, determining which original image it
belongs to.

4.1. Experimental Setting
To construct the MTGL40-5 benchmark, we select eight methods, including four
widely used feature-extraction methods, VGG16 [52], GoogLeNet [53], ResNet50 [54], and
DenseNet121 [55], as well as four geo-localization methods, LCM [56], LPN [49], RK-Net [4],
and FSRA [1]. For a fair comparison, the data processing and experimental settings of all
geo-localization methods are consistent with those in the original papers. Additionally,
all feature-extraction me...

4.2. Evaluation Protocol
(1) Image matching accuracy: In this paper, we rank the image matching process
of geo-localization models using the recall at the top-k accuracy of query images as a
metric, named Recall@k-Query (R@k-Q). Similarly, the accuracy of matching the key and
original images is also evaluated through recall (see the third task of Section 3.2), named
Recall@k-Key (R@k-K) to ensure the accuracy of geo-localization, where k is set to 1.
(2) GPS coordinates average error: For result...

Although the widely used methods have certain advantages over the specifically
designed methods, their low accuracy (less than 60%) in the R@1-Q metric indicates that
there is still significant room for improvement in accurately extracting multi-temporal
features of the same landmark. This highlights the necessity of proposing MTGL40-5 to
explore the cross-time challenge.

4.5. Parametric Analysis of N and GPS Coordinates Calculation
As mentioned above, patch size N is a hyper-parameter that determines the balance
between global and local information in the query and database images. A larger N

captures more global information, such as the overall terrain features such as rivers and
roads. On the other hand, a smaller N focuses on capturing local information, including
more details of land cover. Therefore, to study the impact of patch size N on geo-localization
performance, we change N from 1024 to 4096 (specifically, 1024, 2048, and 4096). Based
on different N, the image numbers of training, validation, and testing sets are listed in

sort the matching scores of the corresponding database images, and choose the top five

the correct top database image. However, certain methods find it difficult to match the
correct database image when some database images exhibit high similarity with the query
image, such as VGG16 with the left query image and FSRA with the right query image.

conclusion.content_preview:
Conclusions
In this paper, a novel dataset for cross-time geo-localization is proposed, named
MTGL40-5. This dataset comprises 40 diverse geographic locations spanning five different
years. For each location, the large-scale original and key images are captured, which contain
the complete and key content of the corresponding location. Subsequently, the key/original
images are split into query/database images for conducting geo-localization. By analyzing
the content difference of the same landmark in different years, we aim to address the crosstime challenge and enhance the practicality of geo-localization. In addition to the traditional
image matching task, we also perform three supplementary tasks, including correcting
the coordinates of the query image, predicting the coordinates of the key image, and
matching the key image with the original image. Finally, we conduct extensive experiments
and construct a strong benchmark with the MTGL40-5 dataset. These findings serve as
evidence fo...

introduction.content_preview:
Introduction

Sánchez

As important earth observation data, remote-sensing (RS) images incorporate sufficient
and diverse land covers and play an essential role in many fields. Exploring the valuable
information within them according to the demands of various applications is always an
open and hot topic. Among diverse interpretation techniques, RS scene classification is
an elemental one, which aims to define semantic labels in line with the contents of RS
scenes [1,2]. The accurate scene classification results are beneficial to different RS tasks
and applications, such as image retrieval [3], land cover classification [4–6], hazard and
environmental monitoring [7,8], resource exploration [9,10], etc.
Over the past decades, extensive methods have been proposed for RS scene classification. In the early stage, the low- and mid-level visual feature descriptors and traditional
machine-learning methods were popular [11,12]. However, since those hand-crafted features cannot fully describe th...

networks (CNNs), many CNN-based methods have been proposed for RS scene classification [13–15]. On the one hand, the intricate land covers within RS scenes can be represented
owing to CNN’s strong feature learning capacity. On the other hand, by integrating feature
learning and classification into an end-to-end framework, the classifiers embedded at the
top of CNNs can follow feature distributions smoothly. Therefore, these CNN-based methods’ performance is excellent. Recently, the transformer [...

We propose a specialized network (i.e., MIEN) and a specific learning algorithm (i.e.,
PLA) to effectively address the challenges posed by noisy labels in RS scene classification tasks. Combining them, not only can the significant features be mined from RS
scenes, but also the adverse effects caused by noisy labels can be whittled down.

To fully explore the contents within RS scenes, TAMSFM is developed, which fuses
the different immediate features learned by ResNet18 in an interactive self-attention
manner. This way, the local, multiscale, and intra-/inter-level global information
hidden in RS scenes can be mined.
MIEN is designed as a dual-branch structure to mitigate the influence of noisy samples at the network architecture level. Two branches have the same sub-networks,
i.e., ResNet18 embedded with TAMSFM. MIEN can instinc...

Related Work
2.1. RS Scene Classification
In this section, we divide the current RS scene classification methods into two groups
according to their backbone model for a short review.
Based on normal CNNs, diverse networks have been proposed and are partitioned
into the first group. Considering the complicated contents within RS scenes, an attentionconsistent network (ACNet) was proposed in [25]. Instead of the individual RS scene, ACNet receives the scene pairs to explore rich information using ResNet and spatial–spectral
attention mechanisms. Then, an attention-consistent scheme is developed to eliminate
information biases. The reported results confirm ACNet’s effectiveness in RS scene classification. A semantic-aware graph network (SAGN) was proposed in [26]. With the help of
pixel features extracted by CNNs, SAGN adaptively captures object-level region features
from RS scenes and establishes unstructured semantic relationships. Combining these three
elements lays the foundation for ...

Related Work
2.1. RS Scene Classification
In this section, we divide the current RS scene classification methods into two groups
according to their backbone model for a short review.
Based on normal CNNs, diverse networks have been proposed and are partitioned
into the first group. Considering the complicated contents within RS scenes, an attentionconsistent network (ACNet) was proposed in [25]. Instead of the individual RS scene, ACNet receives the scene pairs to explore rich information using ...

long-distance relations, a growing number of transformer-based methods have been proposed for various computer vision [16,17] tasks, as well as for diverse RS tasks [37–39].
To simultaneously mine the homogenous and heterogenous information within RS scenes,
a homo-heterogenous transformer learning framework was proposed in [40]. By dividing
RS scenes into regular and irregular patches, rich knowledge hidden in RS scenes can be
mined by the transformer for the downstream classification task. Lv ...

learning (RS-COCL) is proposed in [54] to mitigate the negative influence of noisy labels.
Complementary learning aims to prevent the model from learning incorrect information
from noisy labels, while ordinary learning focuses on reducing the risks of overfitting.
The final model can achieve favorable results under a noisy scenario through a two-stage
training strategy.

The architecture of the classification sub-network is shown in Figure 5. Its backbone is ResNet18, which contains four residual modules. When an RS scene, Ii , is input
into the classification sub-network, it is mapped into feature maps B1i ∈ R H1 ×W1 ×C1 ,
B2i ∈ R H2 ×W2 ×C2 , B3i ∈ R H3 ×W3 ×C3 , and B4i ∈ R H4 ×W4 ×C4 by different residual modules,

residual way.

Like the standard vision transformer (ViT) [36], our CIIT encoder includes a multihead interaction self-attention (MISA) block and a multi-layer perception (MLP) block
(encompassing two fully connected layers and a Gaussian error linear unit). In addition,
the layer normalization (LN) operation exists before each block. Two blocks are connected
in a residual way. For the input, Xi , the first CIIT encoder’s process can be formulated as

4.2.2. Teacher Sub-Network
In general, the above classification sub-network is able to handle the common RS
scene classification tasks. Nonetheless, when there are some noisy samples (i.e., the RS
scenes with incorrect labels), the classification sub-network would be out of work since
the cumulative errors generated by noisy samples have a severe negative effect [63–65].
To overcome this problem, we introduce a teacher sub-network inspired by [66]. It leverages
the temporal process information f...

where θst and θsc represent the teacher and classification sub-networks’ parameters at the s-th
training step, θst −1 denotes the teacher sub-network’s parameters at the (s − 1)-th training
step, and α is a hyper-parameter to smooth this transition. Note that the initial parameters of
the teacher sub-network are replicated from the classification sub-network. In this fashion,
the predictions of the teacher sub-network contain both the knowledge learned in the

current state (transmitted from the classification network) and the information discovered
in the previous states. Therefore, these predictions may differ from the outputs of the
classification sub-network, which would be caused by the noisy samples. By investigating
these different clues, the adverse impacts of noisy samples on the classification sub-network
can be weakened.
4.3. Progressive Learning Algorithm
The developed PLA consists of three stages, i.e., the DNL, APL, and ESL stages. In th...

If Ii is a clean sample, the above label selection strategy ensures the complementary
label is irrelevant to Ii . If Ii is a noisy sample, the combination of yi and yit can also support
the label selection strategy to find an irrelevant label.
Then, the negative-learning (NL) loss function [67] is introduced to train the classification sub-network using the irrelevant scene and label pairs. Its definition is
c

the NL loss function, our MIEN can learn that RS scenes do not belong to certain categories.
Correspondingly, the relations between RS scenes and their “true” labels can be explored.

4.3.2. Adaptively Positive-Learning Stage
After the DNL stage, MIEN can distinguish the RS scenes not belonging to certain
categories. Now, we want to connect RS scenes and their “true” labels. To achieve this,
we first design an adaptive clean sample selection scheme. For an RS scene, Ii , suppose
its manual label and two sub-networks’ predictions are yi , yic , and yit . If yi = yic = yit , Ii
can be regarded as a clean sample and otherwise as a noisy sample. The reasonableness of
this simple ...

4.3.3. Exhaustive Soft-Label Learning
After the DNL and APL stages, the negative and positive relationships between RS
scenes and the corresponding semantics are partially explored. To further and comprehensively mine the valuable information hidden in RS scenes, we developed the ESL stage at
the end of the progressive learning algorithm.

5.5. Comparison with Other Classification Models
For RS scene classification with noisy labels, the classification results are decided by the
robustness of the learning algorithm and the ability of the feature extractor. In Section 5.2,
we demonstrated the effectiveness of our PLA noise tolerance learning algorithm by only
using ResNet18 as the backbone of MIEN (i.e., Res-PLA). In this section, we choose several
networks designed for RS scenes as the backbones of the classification and teacher s...

A, and MIEN-R, respectively. The above models are trained using PLA, and the backbones’
parameters are set according to the original literature. The OA values of different MIENs
under the scenario of NR = 0.1 are summarized in Table 7. In addition, Giga floating point
operations (GFLOPs) values of different MIENs are provided for reference.
Table 7. OA values (%) and GFLOPs of different MIENs trained by PLA counted on three data sets
(NR = 0.1). The best values are marked in bold.

By observing the results, we can see that our MIEN achieves the best OA values
on UCM21 and the second-best OA values on AID30 and NWPU45. This proves the
superiority of MIEN’s feature extraction capabilities and also reflects that MIEN still has
room for improvement when facing large-scale complex RS scene data sets such as AID30
and NWPU45. Although our MIEN is not optimal in OA, its GFLOPs are the best, showing
that it is relatively lightweight. In summary, our MIEN is efficient and energetic...

6. Discussion
In this section, we further analyze the differences, advantages, disadvantages, and future directions of our MIEN-PLA in comparison to similar methods.
6.1. Similar Work Comparison
In recent years, noisy RS scene classification has been a hot research topic, and many
successful methods have been proposed [50–54,67,73–75]. Among them, CMR-NLD [50],
RS-COCL [54], and NLNL [67] are similar to our methods as they are also aimed at reducing
the impact of noisy labels on the model from d...

other methods can be summarized as follows. CMR-NLD, RS-COCL, and NLNL focus on
filtering out noisy scenes during training, whereas our methods are aimed at discovering
the possible noisy data and relabeling them. This way, all samples can be used to train the
model, preventing information loss. Meanwhile, compared to two RS-oriented methods
(CMR-NLD and RS-COCL), which only use the existing CNNs to generate the visual
features, our methods develop a specific network (i.e., MIEN) to explore the ...

models. To overcome the above limitations, we develop

designed. It encloses two parts, i.e., a data partition scheme and

II. R ELATED W ORK
In this section, we review the published works from the
following aspects, i.e., HSI classification, learning with noisy
labels, and HSI classification with noisy labels.
A. HSI Classification
Nowadays, CNNs and their variants have dominated the
HSI classification community. Benefiting from the stacked
structure and nonlinear fitting talents, CNNs can explore the
spatial–spectral information from HSIs for accurate classification results [30], [31], [32], [33]. Considering the cube
properties of HSIs, a 3-D octave convolution network with
a spatial–spectral attention mechanism was proposed in [34].
The authors first expand the original octave convolution into
a 3-D version to explore the spatial–spectral clues from
HSIs with a relatively time cost. Then, the spatial–spectral
attention mechanism is developed to enhance the obtained
features mutually. The reported results counted on four HSIs
confirm its usefulness. Lv et al. [35] intro...

This matrix indicates whether pairs of pixels are similar or
not. Following [68], we measure the similarity of pixel pairs
by computing the inner product of the probability vectors of
two pixels, i.e., Suv = puT · pv , which can be regarded as the
predicted similarity posterior probability. Thus, the similarity
prediction issue can be transformed into a binary classification
problem. Therefore, the similarity loss is expressed as follows:
2n

1 XX
Auv log Suv + (1 − Auv ) log(1 − Suv ).
(2n)2 u=1 u̸=v
(10)

The similarity loss ensures that pixels from homogenous
regions have similar probability distributions and enhances the
information interaction between the two networks. In this way,
the knowledge covered by the noisy samples can be mined by
our SPTNet. Moreover, since this loss is label-independent,
it is robust to label noise inherently.
Based on the above discussion, the final loss functions for
two SPTNets are
L1 = λs Ls + (1 − λs )L1CE
L2 = λs Ls + (1 − λs )L2CE

where λs is a free parameter to balance the importance of the
CE loss and similarity loss. In the test phase, we average the
predictions of the two models as the final prediction to obtain
more refined results.

section is summarized in Algorithm 1.

This article proposes a simple yet effective network SPTNet
and a noise-tolerant learning algorithm for the HSI classification task with noisy labels. SPTNet first analyzes the
spectral information within HSIs by combining 2-D CNN
and 3-D CNN. Then, a new SPT is developed based on th...

Therefore, a growing number of studies have been proposed to
explore the usefulness of Mamba in HSI classification. Nevertheless, most of them only apply Mamba to HSIs directly but do not
consider the inherent characteristics of HSIs properly. To exploit
the potential of Mamba in HSI classification deeply, this article
presents a new spatial–spectral complementary Mamba with a
spatial spiral scan named SpiralMamba. It mainly encloses three
main components: a spatial Mamba encoder (SpaME), a spectral
Mamba encoder (SpeME), and a spatial–spectral complementary

Index Terms— Hyperspectral image (HSI) classification, selective structured state space model (SSM) (Mamba).

YPERSPECTRAL image (HSI) processing is always
a hot topic in the remote sensing (RS) community.
Unlike the regular RS products, HSIs contain rich and nearly
continuous spectral information [1], [2]. They consist of hundreds of narrow bands with different manifestations on diverse
objects [3]. Therefore, HSIs can reflect the physical properties
of objects at a fine-grained level and provide richer information
for Earth observation [4], [5]. Owing to this property, HSIs
can be applied to a wide ra...

Due to this shortcoming, its generalization in practical applications is limited. Therefore, there is a need to develop an HSI
classification method that can discover valuable HSIs clues
with a relatively low computational complexity.
Recently, with the emergence and popularity of the selective
structured state space model (SSM) (Mamba) [39], [40],
a favorable solution to the need mentioned above has become
available. Like Transformers, Mamba has strong global modeling capabilities. Unlike Trans...

adequately fuse the spatial and spectral features. SSCFM
involves a relationship learning block (RLB) and a threshold
enhancement mechanism (TEM), which aim to learn the
complex relationships between different features and refine the
learned relationship weights. The spatial and spectral features
can be integrated accordingly in this progressive learning and
optimization manner. It is important to note that the choice

and widen the network. This way, the discriminative local

C. Spectral Mamba Encoder
SpeME aims to extract complex spectral features from the
global and local perspectives. As shown in Fig. 1, SpeME
consists of a spectral bidirectional Mamba (SpeBiM), an MLC,
two LN layers, and two residual modules. Its formula is
expressed as follows:
XBiM = SpeBiM(LN(Xemb )) + Xemb
Fspe = MLC(LN(XBiM )) + XBiM

where XBiM and Fspe represent the outputs of SpeBiM and
SpeME, and SpeBiM(·), MLC(·), and LN(·) denote the functions of SpeBiM, MLC, and LN, respectively. Here, SpeBiM
is used to model the global long-term dependencies between
spectral tokens, and MLC focuses on extracting local features
within spectral tokens. With the cooperation of SpeBiM and
MLC, SpeME can encode high-dimensional spectral features
comprehensively and accurately.

change detection

paragraphs.text:
Abstract— Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) image change
detection is a hot but challenging task due to SAR images’
complex contents and inherent speckle noises. The expected
change detection

methods.content_preview:
methods should reduce the influence of speckle
noises, obtain the discriminative feature representations, and
generate accurate change maps simultaneously. To these ends,
we propose a new SAR image change detection method named
feature fusion of information transfer network (FFITN). First,
we develop a hybrid convolution block (HCB) to depress the
speckle noise impacts and explore the valuable information from
SAR images. Thus, the feature extraction module (FEM) is constructed to obtain the multilevel features. Then, an information
transfer module (ITM) is proposed to capture the salient regions
from various aspects. Also, the salient knowledge is transferred
among features at different levels to enhance their discrimination.
Next, a self-attention-based feature fusion module (SAFFM)
is introduced to fuse various features. Finally, a change map
generation module (CMGM) with the clustering algorithm and
specific loss functions is designed to produce the pseudo labels
and change maps.

RS interpretation task, RS change detection is always an active
research topic that aims to identify the changes that occurred
in multitemporal RS images, which cover the same geographic
areas and are collected at different times [1], [2]. Among

discussion.content_preview:
discussion, we combine CNNs and Transformers
and propose a new W-shaped dual-Siamese branch hierarchical
network for HRRS image CD named W-shaped hierarchical
network (WNet). WNet first incorporates a Siamese CNN and
a Siamese Transformer into a dual-branch encoder to extract
multilevel local fine-grained features and global long-range contextual dependencies. Also, we introduce deformable ideas into
the Siamese CNN and Transformer to make WNet understand
the critical and irregular areas within HRRS images. Second,
the difference enhancement module (DEM) is developed and
embedded into the encoder to produce the difference feature
maps at different levels. Using simple pixel-wise subtraction
and channel-wise concatenation, the changes of interest and
irrelevant changes can be highlighted and suppressed in a
learnable manner. Next, the multilevel difference feature maps
are fused stage by stage by CNN–Transformer fusion modules
(CTFMs), which are the basic units of the decoder in WNet.

HANGE detection (CD) is one of the significant topics
in remote-sensing (RS) image processing, which aims
at detecting changes of interest in bitemporal images covering the same regions [1]. It can be used in various RS
applications, such as land cover recognition [2], urban expansion estimation [3], disaster assessment [4], and agriculture
monitoring [5]. With the development of sensors, many highresolution (HR) RS images can be generated daily. These
images bring profitable information and for...

The main contributions of our work can be summarized as
follows.
1) An end-to-end HRRS image CD model named WNet is
developed in this article. WNet is a hierarchical network
with a dual-Siamese branch encoder, which involves a

deformable ideas into the dual-Siamese branch encoder.

(in Transformers) can be mitigated.

the dual-Siamese branch encoder. By combining simple
subtraction and concatenation operations, the difference
feature maps can be obtained in an efficient...

III. M ETHODOLOGY
A. Overall Framework
The overall framework of our WNet is shown in Fig. 3,
which consists of a dual-branch encoder and a fusion decoder.
When we input two bitemporal HRRS images I1 ∈ R H ×W ×C
and I2 ∈ R H ×W ×C into WNet, they will be passed through
the dual-branch encoder first, which encloses a Siamese CNN
branch and a Siamese Transformer branch. Thus, the multilevel local fine-grained information {(Fi1 , Fi2 ), i = 1, 2, 3, 4}
and the global context dependencies {(Gi1 , Gi2...
Authorized licensed use limited to: XIDIAN UNIVERSITY. Downloaded on April 16,2026 at 05:56:00 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

Fig. 3. Framework of the proposed WNet. Multilevel features are extracted from bitemporal HRRS image pairs through Siamese CNN and Siamese
Transformer branches. Then, the difference feature maps are generated by DEMs at different stages. After that, the local, global, and cross-stage information
is fused using CTFMs stage by stage. Finally, a simple classifier is designed to generate change maps.

In addition, skip connections are applied to connect the
encoder and the decoder at different levels to ensure the
stability of WNet.
B. Dual-Siamese Branch Encoder

of the dual-branch encoder are a Siamese CNN and a Siamese
Transformer. Both of them leverage DEM to generate difference feature maps at various levels. Thus, we first explain
DEM in this section. The structure of DEM is exhibited in

All experiments are conducted on a single NVIDIA Tesla
V100 with 32G video memory, and the compilation platform
is PyTorch. The common data augmentation schemes (e.g.,
horizontal/vertical flipping, scale cropping, and blurring) are
used in the training phase to ensure the WNet’s stability.
The dual-branch backbones of WNet are initialized by the
pretrained parameters of ResNet18 [33] and DAT-tiny [31]
using ImageNet. Other parts of our WNet are initialized
randomly. Here...
